selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":84,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["gardening","hiking","birdwatching"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":4,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":92,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":30,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

INPUT:
mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":84,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["gardening","hiking","birdwatching"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":4,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":92,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":30,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I believe that the best way to handle fake content on social media is to simply not engage with it or give it any credibility. I spend only an hour a day on there, mostly looking at posts from friends and family who share their daily lives, like what they're doing in the garden or hiking adventures. With my high agreeableness score, I find that maintaining positive interactions is key for me. So when I see something that seems untrue—whether it's misinformation about current events or someone posting fake pictures to get attention—I just scroll past it without saying anything unless it directly affects people I know personally. My conscientiousness also plays a role because I want to make sure my own posts are factual and respectful. Over the years, being an atheist native-born American in Philadelphia has taught me not everyone sees things the same way, but that doesn't mean they're right either.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I start by acknowledging that misinformation can be overwhelming if you try to address every single post. Since I'm retired and spend limited time on social media—around one hour daily—I focus on curating a feed of trusted sources rather than engaging with the noise. My high agreeableness makes me want to keep things positive, so I avoid confrontation unless necessary for public safety.",
  "tone": "Calm, reflective, and measured",
  "citations": ["Personal experience"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I believe that the best way to handle fake content on social media is to simply not engage with it or give it any credibility. I spend only an hour a day on there, mostly looking at posts from friends and family who share their daily lives, like what they're doing in the garden or hiking adventures. With my high agreeableness score, I find that maintaining positive interactions is key for me. So when I see something that seems untrue—whether it's misinformation about current events or someone posting fake pictures to get attention—I just scroll past it without saying anything unless it directly affects people I know personally. My conscientiousness also plays a role because I want to make sure my own posts are factual and respectful. Over the years, being an atheist native-born American in Philadelphia has taught me not everyone sees things the same way, but that doesn't mean they're right either.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I start by acknowledging that misinformation can be overwhelming if you try to address every single post. Since I'm retired and spend limited time on social media—around one hour daily—I focus on curating a feed of trusted sources rather than engaging with the noise. My high agreeableness makes me want to keep things positive, so I avoid confrontation unless necessary for public safety.
